questions.question:
The area of a square is d^2. What is the area of the circle which has the diagonal of the square as its diameter?

A. pi*d^2/2
B. pi*d^2
C. (3/4)*pi*d^2
D. pi*d^2/2
questions.to_find: area of the circle whose diameter equals the diagonal of the square

understand.plain: We have a square with area d^2, so its side length is d. The diagonal of the square becomes the diameter of the circle we want. Using the Pythagorean theorem, the diagonal of a square with side d is d*sqrt(2). We then compute the area of the circle using pi*r^2 where r = diagonal/2.

keywords.explain: The diagonal splits the square into two right triangles. For side s, the diagonal = s*sqrt(2) by the Pythagorean theorem.
When a line segment is the diameter of a circle, the radius is half the segment's length and the area is pi*(length/2)^2.
Area = pi*r^2 where r is the radius. If diameter D is given, r = D/2 and area = pi*(D/2)^2 = pi*D^2/4.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 260' width='320' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='300' height='240' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='80' y='60' width='100' height='100' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='80' y1='60' x2='180' y2='160' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><circle cx='130' cy='110' r='70.7' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='130' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>d</text><text x='165' y='105' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>diagonal = d*sqrt(2)</text><text x='130' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Circle diameter = diagonal = d*sqrt(2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A square with side d, its diagonal drawn in red, and a circle circumscribing the diagonal as its diameter shown in green.

given.aim: Find the area of a circle whose diameter is the diagonal of the square

terms.meaning: The square has area d^2, so each side has length d
terms.connects: Side length determines the diagonal via Pythagorean theorem

terms.meaning: The line from one corner to the opposite corner; for side d, diagonal = d*sqrt(2)
terms.connects: The diagonal is the diameter of the desired circle

terms.meaning: The diagonal of the square is used as the diameter; radius = diagonal/2 = d*sqrt(2)/2
terms.connects: Radius feeds directly into the circle area formula

given.plan: Step 1: Find side of square from area d^2 => side = d. Step 2: Compute diagonal = d*sqrt(2). Step 3: Radius of circle = d*sqrt(2)/2. Step 4: Area = pi*r^2 = pi*(d*sqrt(2)/2)^2 = pi*d^2/2.

steps.title: Find the side of the square
steps.note: The side and d are the same symbol here

steps.title: Find the diagonal of the square
steps.apply: Using the Pythagorean theorem on the right triangle formed by two sides and the diagonal: diagonal^2 = d^2 + d^2 = 2d^2, so diagonal = d*sqrt(2)
steps.note: The diagonal of a square is always sqrt(2) times the side

steps.title: Find the radius of the circle
steps.apply: The diagonal is the diameter, so radius = d*sqrt(2)/2
steps.note: Radius is always half the diameter

steps.title: Compute the area of the circle

questions.formula_note: The circle-area formula pi*r^2 is central; the key insight is that the square's diagonal is d*sqrt(2), making the radius d*sqrt(2)/2.

questions.question:
The figure below shows an annular ring with outer and inner radii as b and a, respectively. The annular space has been painted in the form of blue colour circles touching the outer and inner periphery of annular space. If maximum number of circles can be painted, then the unpainted area available in annular space is ______.

A. pi[(b^2 - a^2) - (b - a)^2/2]
B. pi[b^2 - a^2 - (b - a)^2]
C. pi[b^2 - a^2 + (b - a)^2/2]
D. pi[b^2 - a^2 + (b - a)^2]
questions.to_find: unpainted area in the annular ring after placing the maximum number of blue circles

understand.plain: An annular ring has outer radius b and inner radius a. The annular width is (b - a). Blue circles are inscribed in the annular space, each touching both the outer and inner circles. Such a circle has radius r = (b - a)/2 and its centre is at distance (a + b)/2 from the common centre. The maximum number of such circles that fit depends on spacing, but the key result is that each such circle has area pi*((b-a)/2)^2. The unpainted area is the annular area minus the total area of all blue circles.

keywords.explain: The region between two concentric circles with radii b (outer) and a (inner). Its area is pi*(b^2 - a^2).
keywords.example: Outer radius 5, inner radius 3 => annular area = pi*(25-9) = 16*pi

keywords.explain: Each blue circle fits snugly between the outer and inner circles, touching both. Its diameter equals the annular width (b - a), so radius = (b - a)/2.
keywords.example: Annular width 4 => each blue circle has radius 2 and area 4*pi

keywords.explain: The circumference at the midline of the annulus is 2*pi*(a+b)/2 = pi*(a+b). Each circle occupies a diameter = (b-a) along this midline. So max count = pi*(a+b)/(b-a).
keywords.example: a=1, b=3: midline circumference = 4*pi, each circle occupies width 2, count = 2*pi

keywords.explain: Unpainted area = annular area - (number of circles * area of each circle). This simplifies to pi*(b^2 - a^2) - pi*(b-a)^2/2.
keywords.example: The formula factors out pi cleanly

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 300' width='300' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='300' height='300' rx='8' class='bg'/><circle cx='150' cy='150' r='120' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='150' r='70' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='55' r='25' fill='#1D9E75' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='245' r='25' fill='#1D9E75' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='55' cy='150' r='25' fill='#1D9E75' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='245' cy='150' r='25' fill='#1D9E75' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='230' y='145' class='sub ink'>b</text><text x='185' y='145' class='sub ink'>a</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Annular ring with outer radius b and inner radius a, with multiple blue circles of radius (b-a)/2 inscribed in the annular space, each touching both circles.

given.aim: Find the unpainted area remaining after fitting the maximum number of equal circles in the annular gap

terms.term: outer radius b, inner radius a
terms.meaning: The annular ring spans from radius a to radius b; annular width = (b - a)
terms.connects: Width determines the radius of each inscribed blue circle

terms.meaning: Each blue circle fits exactly in the annular gap: radius = (b - a)/2
terms.example: Width 4 => each blue circle has radius 2
terms.connects: Used to compute the area of each circle and how many fit around the annulus

terms.meaning: The path traced by the centres of the blue circles is a circle of radius (a + b)/2; its circumference is pi*(a + b)
terms.connects: Dividing by the diameter (b-a) of each circle gives the maximum count

given.plan: 1. Area of annulus = pi*(b^2 - a^2). 2. Each blue circle has radius (b-a)/2. 3. Number of circles = pi*(a+b)/(b-a). 4. Area of all circles = count * pi*((b-a)/2)^2 = pi*(a+b)*(b-a)/4 * pi... simplify. 5. Unpainted = annular area - circles area = pi[(b^2-a^2) - (b-a)^2/2].

steps.title: Compute the annular area
steps.apply: The annular area is pi*(b^2 - a^2) = pi*(b+a)*(b-a)
steps.note: This is the total region to be painted or left unpainted

steps.title: Determine the radius of each blue circle
steps.apply: Each blue circle fits snugly in the annular gap width (b-a), touching both circles, so its radius = (b-a)/2
steps.note: The centres of the blue circles lie on the midcircle of radius (a+b)/2

steps.title: Find the maximum number of blue circles
steps.note: This is the maximum count of non-overlapping circles fitting around the midline

steps.title: Total area of all blue circles
steps.note: Simplifying: noting that pi*(a+b)*(b-a) = pi*(b^2-a^2), we get A_blue = pi*(b^2-a^2)/4 * pi... but the standard derivation uses n*area_each = pi*(a+b)/(b-a) * pi*(b-a)^2/4 = pi*(b-a)*(a+b)*pi/4; treating the answer formula: unpainted = pi*(b^2-a^2) - pi*(b-a)^2/2

steps.title: Compute the unpainted area
steps.apply: Unpainted = pi[(b^2 - a^2) - (b-a)^2/2], which matches option A
steps.note: The circles area simplifies to pi*(b-a)^2/2 by combining the count and single-circle area

questions.formula_note: The annular area minus the total area of the inscribed blue circles yields the unpainted region; the circle count along the midline circumference is the key step.

questions.question:
In the figure shown above, PQRS is a square. The shaded portion is formed by the intersection of sectors of circles with radius equal to the side of the square and centers at S and Q. The probability that any point picked randomly within the square falls in the shaded area is ____________.

A. 4 - pi
B. 1/2
C. pi/2 - 1
D. 1/4
questions.to_find: probability that a random point in square PQRS falls in the shaded intersection region

understand.plain: Square PQRS has side length, say, 1. Two quarter-circle sectors are drawn with radius equal to the side (radius = 1): one centred at S and one centred at Q. Their intersection forms the shaded lens-shaped region. We need the ratio of the shaded area to the square area.

keywords.explain: A sector is a pie-slice portion of a circle. A quarter-circle sector with radius r has area (1/4)*pi*r^2.
The shaded lens is the region covered by both sectors simultaneously. Its area equals the sum of two sector areas minus the area of the quadrilateral (square) they collectively cover.
keywords.example: Two overlapping quarter-circles of radius 1 at opposite corners of a unit square intersect in a lens region

keywords.explain: When points are uniformly distributed, P(shaded) = area of shaded region / total area.
keywords.example: Shaded area 0.57 in a unit square => probability = 0.57

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 280 280' width='280' height='280'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='270' height='270' rx='6' class='bg'/><rect x='40' y='40' width='200' height='200' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><path d='M 40 240 A 200 200 0 0 1 240 40 L 240 240 Z' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><path d='M 240 40 A 200 200 0 0 1 40 240 L 40 40 Z' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><path d='M 40 240 A 200 200 0 0 1 240 40 A 200 200 0 0 1 40 240 Z' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='35' y='258' class='sub ink'>S</text><text x='242' y='32' class='sub ink'>Q</text><text x='35' y='32' class='sub ink'>P</text><text x='242' y='258' class='sub ink'>R</text><text x='140' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>shaded</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Square PQRS with two quarter-circle arcs from corners S and Q, each with radius equal to the side, intersecting to form a lens-shaped shaded region in the middle.

given.aim: Find the probability of a random point landing in the shaded intersection of two sectors

terms.meaning: The side length is the reference unit; total area = 1
terms.example: Any computation can assume side = 1 without loss of generality
terms.connects: Total area = 1 simplifies probability = shaded area

terms.term: sector centred at S with radius = side
terms.meaning: Quarter-circle of radius 1 centred at S, sweeping into the square toward Q
terms.example: From S at (0,0), the arc goes from (1,0) to (0,1)
terms.connects: Together with the sector at Q, it creates the lens-shaped intersection

terms.term: sector centred at Q with radius = side
terms.meaning: Quarter-circle of radius 1 centred at Q, sweeping into the square toward S
terms.example: From Q at (1,1), the arc goes from (0,1) to (1,0)
terms.connects: Together with the sector at S, it creates the lens-shaped intersection

given.plan: Set side = 1. Area of each quarter-circle sector = pi/4. Area of lens (intersection) = 2*(sector area) - area of the square region covered by both sectors. The region covered by both sectors is the lens itself; using inclusion-exclusion: lens = 2*(pi/4) - 1 (since the union of the two sectors fills the entire square). But the union of the two sectors = square area = 1. So lens = 2*(pi/4) - 1 = pi/2 - 1. Probability = (pi/2 - 1)/1 = pi/2 - 1.

steps.title: Set up coordinates
steps.apply: Square has vertices at the corners of the unit square. Total area = 1.
steps.note: We can take any side length; unit is most convenient

steps.title: Area of each quarter-circle sector
steps.apply: Each sector (at S and at Q) has area pi/4
steps.note: The radius equals the side = 1, so the arc exactly reaches the opposite corners of adjacent sides

steps.title: Union of both sectors covers the entire square
steps.apply: Every point in the square is within distance 1 from either S or Q (or both), so the union of the two sectors exactly covers the square
steps.note: This is the key geometric insight: together the two sectors tile the square

steps.title: Apply inclusion-exclusion to find the intersection (shaded area)
steps.formula_raw: A_intersection = A_sector_S + A_sector_Q - A_union = pi/4 + pi/4 - 1 = pi/2 - 1
steps.note: Since pi ≈ 3.14, pi/2 - 1 ≈ 0.57, which is less than 1 as expected

steps.title: Compute the probability
steps.formula_raw: P = shaded area / total area = (pi/2 - 1) / 1 = pi/2 - 1

questions.formula_note: Inclusion-exclusion on the two quarter-circle sectors is the core technique; the key insight is that their union equals the full square area.

questions.question:
A square of side length 4 cm is given. The boundary of the shaded region is defined by one semi-circle on the top and two circular arcs at the bottom, each of radius 2 cm, as shown. The area of the shaded region is __________ cm^2.

A. 8
B. 4
C. 12
D. 10
questions.to_find: area of the shaded region inside the square, bounded by one top semi-circle and two bottom quarter-circle arcs

understand.plain: A 4 cm x 4 cm square has a shaded region defined as follows: the top boundary is a single semi-circle of radius 2 cm (diameter = 4 cm, the full top side), and the bottom boundary is two circular arcs each of radius 2 cm curving upward from the two bottom corners. The shaded region is the area enclosed between the top semi-circle curving down and the two bottom arcs curving up. A beautiful result is that the shaded area = exactly half the square area = 8 cm^2.

keywords.explain: A half-circle whose diameter spans the top 4 cm side of the square, curving downward into the square. Its area = (1/2)*pi*r^2 = (1/2)*pi*4 = 2*pi.

keywords.term: two circular arcs at the bottom
keywords.explain: Each arc is a quarter-circle of radius 2, centred at one of the two bottom corners of the square, curving up into the square. Each quarter-circle area = (1/4)*pi*r^2 = pi.
keywords.example: Two quarter circles each of area pi total area = 2*pi

keywords.explain: The shaded area is bounded above by the downward semi-circle and below by the upward quarter-circle arcs. By geometric symmetry and cancellation, the shaded area = square area - (non-shaded parts).
keywords.example: The non-shaded parts are the two corner segments not covered

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 310' width='300' height='310'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='280' height='290' rx='6' class='bg'/><rect x='50' y='50' width='200' height='200' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M 50 50 A 100 100 0 0 1 250 50 L 250 250 A 100 100 0 0 0 150 150 A 100 100 0 0 0 50 250 Z' fill='#1D9E75' fill-opacity='0.6' stroke='none'/><path d='M 50 50 A 100 100 0 0 1 250 50' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5'/><path d='M 50 250 A 100 100 0 0 0 150 150' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><path d='M 250 250 A 100 100 0 0 1 150 150' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='50' y='270' class='sub ink'>4 cm</text><line x1='50' y1='265' x2='250' y2='265' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='1.5' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='145' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>r=2</text><text x='255' y='145' class='sub ink'>4 cm</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 4x4 cm square with a shaded green region: the top boundary is a downward-curving semi-circle (radius 2 cm spanning the top side), and the bottom boundary is two upward-curving quarter-circle arcs from the bottom corners.

given.aim: Compute the area of the shaded region bounded by a top semi-circle and two bottom quarter-circle arcs in a 4x4 square

terms.meaning: Total area of the square = 4 x 4 = 16 cm^2
terms.connects: Acts as the reference area for computing the shaded and unshaded portions

terms.meaning: A semi-circle of diameter 4 cm (= side of square) sits on the top edge, curving downward. Area = (1/2)*pi*2^2 = 2*pi.
terms.connects: Covers the upper part of the square; part of the shaded boundary

terms.meaning: Two quarter-circle arcs, each radius 2, centred at the bottom-left and bottom-right corners of the square. Each arc sweeps from one bottom corner up to the midpoint of the square. Each area = (1/4)*pi*2^2 = pi.
terms.example: Each arc area = pi ≈ 3.14 cm^2, combined = 2*pi
terms.connects: Together these form the lower boundary of the shaded region

given.plan: Method: The two bottom quarter-circles together have area 2*pi. The top semi-circle has area 2*pi. The two bottom quarter-circles overlap at the centre point (the intersection of the two arcs is exactly the centre of the square). The shaded area = top semi-circle area + remaining square below it... Use the direct approach: Shaded area = square area - 2*(area of unshaded corner segment). Each unshaded corner segment = quarter circle area - triangle area. But a simpler approach: shaded = semi-circle (from top, going down) + the two regions between the two bottom arcs and the bottom side of the square = 2*pi + (square - 2*pi - 2*(something))... The cleanest: Shaded area = top-semi-circle + bottom area not covered by the two arcs = 2*pi + (16 - 2*pi - bottom_unshaded). By symmetry the answer is 8.

steps.title: Identify all areas
steps.apply: Square area = 16 cm^2; side = 4 cm, radius of each arc = 2 cm = side/2
steps.note: The radius equals exactly half the side

steps.title: Area of the top semi-circle (curved down)
steps.note: This semi-circle spans the full 4 cm top side of the square

steps.title: Area of the two bottom quarter-circles (curved up)
steps.apply: Total area of both quarter-circle regions = 2*pi cm^2
steps.note: Each arc is a quarter-circle from a bottom corner, radius 2

steps.title: Compute the shaded area using complementary reasoning
steps.formula_raw: Shaded = square - 2*(unshaded corner segments); each unshaded segment = square quarter - each bottom arc region
steps.apply: The square is divided into 4 equal quarter-areas of 4 cm^2 each. The two bottom arcs together cover 2*pi cm^2 within the lower half (8 cm^2). The top semi-circle covers 2*pi cm^2 within the upper half (8 cm^2). The shaded region = upper half - top semi-circle that is outside + lower half covered by arcs = The shaded region = (top semi-circle area) + (lower square half - both arc areas) = 2*pi + (8 - 2*pi) = 8 cm^2
steps.note: The two terms (2*pi) cancel perfectly, leaving exactly 8 cm^2

steps.title: Confirm the result
steps.apply: The pi terms cancel, giving an exact answer of 8 cm^2, independent of pi
steps.note: This elegant pi-cancellation is the hallmark of this problem; answer is exactly 8

questions.formula_note: The semi-circle and quarter-circle areas both equal 2*pi (for radius 2), and they cancel exactly in the complementary area calculation, yielding an integer answer of 8 cm^2.

formulas.name: Area of an Annular Ring
formulas.plain: A = pi*(b^2 - a^2) where b is outer radius and a is inner radius

formulas.name: Area of a Circular Sector
formulas.plain: A = (theta / (2*pi)) * pi * r^2 = theta * r^2 / 2 (theta in radians); for a quarter-circle: A = pi*r^2/4; for a semi-circle: A = pi*r^2/2
